preamble.summary: Isaiah 58 is one of the most socially explosive chapters in the prophetic canon. The people fast and wonder why God does not notice. God answers: their fasting is a performance of piety while they exploit their workers and quarrel with fists. The true fast God chooses is justice — loosing bonds of wickedness, feeding the hungry, housing the homeless, clothing the naked. When worship becomes justice, then 'your light shall break forth like the dawn.' The chapter closes with a call to Sabbath delight that reframes rest as joy rather than obligation.
preamble.remarkable: Verses 6-7 constitute one of the most direct statements of social ethics in the Hebrew Bible: the fast God desires is not abstaining from food but abstaining from injustice. Jesus echoes this priority in Matthew 25:35-36 ('I was hungry and you gave Me food'). Verse 8's 'your light shall break forth like the dawn' becomes a controlling image for the entire final section of Isaiah.
preamble.friction: The tension between ritual and ethics is not either/or — Isaiah does not abolish fasting but redefines it. We have rendered the passage to preserve this nuance: God does not reject worship but refuses worship severed from justice.
preamble.connections: Verses 6-7 anticipate Jesus' parable of the sheep and goats (Matt 25:31-46). The 'light breaking forth' (v.8) connects to Isaiah 60:1 and John 1:5. The Sabbath theology of vv.13-14 anticipates Jesus' Sabbath controversies (Mark 2:27-28). James 1:27 echoes this chapter's definition of true religion.

verses.text_hebrew: קְרָא בְגָרוֹן אַל־תַּחְשֹׂךְ כַּשּׁוֹפָר הָרֵם קוֹלֶךָ וְהַגֵּד לְעַמִּי פִּשְׁעָם וּלְבֵית יַעֲקֹב חַטֹּאתָם
verses.text_kjv: Cry aloud, spare not, lift up thy voice like a trumpet, and shew my people their transgression, and the house of Jacob their sins.
verses.rendering:
Cry aloud — do not hold back!
Lift up your voice like a shofar!
Declare to My people their transgression,
to the house of Jacob their sins.
translator_notes: 'Shofar' is retained rather than 'trumpet' — the ram's horn carries covenantal and liturgical weight that 'trumpet' flattens.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאוֹתִי יוֹם יוֹם יִדְרֹשׁוּן וְדַעַת דְּרָכַי יֶחְפָּצוּן כְּגוֹי אֲשֶׁר־צְדָקָה עָשָׂה וּמִשְׁפַּט אֱלֹהָיו לֹא עָזָב יִשְׁאָלוּנִי מִשְׁפְּטֵי־צֶדֶק קִרְבַת אֱלֹהִים יֶחְפָּצוּן
verses.text_kjv: Yet they seek me daily, and delight to know my ways, as a nation that did righteousness, and forsook not the ordinance of their God: they ask of me the ordinances of justice; they take delight in approaching to God.
verses.rendering:
Yet day after day they seek Me
and delight to know My ways,
as if they were a nation that practiced righteousness
and had not forsaken the justice of their God.
They ask Me for righteous judgments;
they delight in drawing near to God.
translator_notes: The 'as if' (ke-goi asher) is devastating — they perform the posture of faithfulness while living in contradiction to it.

verses.text_hebrew: לָמָּה צַּמְנוּ וְלֹא רָאִיתָ עִנִּינוּ נַפְשֵׁנוּ וְלֹא תֵדָע הֵן בְּיוֹם צֹמְכֶם תִּמְצְאוּ־חֵפֶץ וְכָל־עַצְּבֵיכֶם תִּנְגֹּשׂוּ
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore have we fasted, say they, and thou seest not? wherefore have we afflicted our soul, and thou takest no knowledge? Behold, in the day of your fast ye find pleasure, and exact all your labours.
verses.rendering:
'Why have we fasted, and You do not see?
Why have we humbled ourselves, and You take no notice?'
Look — on the day of your fast you pursue your own interests
and oppress all your workers.
translator_notes: God quotes the people's complaint, then immediately exposes the fraud: their fasting coexists with economic exploitation.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵן לְרִיב וּמַצָּה תָּצוּמוּ וּלְהַכּוֹת בְּאֶגְרֹף רֶשַׁע לֹא־תָצוּמוּ כַיּוֹם לְהַשְׁמִיעַ בַּמָּרוֹם קוֹלְכֶם
verses.text_kjv: Behold, ye fast for strife and debate, and to smite with the fist of wickedness: ye shall not fast as ye do this day, to make your voice to be heard on high.
verses.rendering:
You fast only to quarrel and fight
and to strike with a wicked fist.
You do not fast as you do today
to make your voice heard on high.
translator_notes: The irony: fasting is supposed to humble, but their fast days produce quarreling and violence.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲכָזֶה יִהְיֶה צוֹם אֶבְחָרֵהוּ יוֹם עַנּוֹת אָדָם נַפְשׁוֹ הֲלָכֹף כְּאַגְמֹן רֹאשׁוֹ וְשַׂק וָאֵפֶר יַצִּיעַ הֲלָזֶה תִּקְרָא־צוֹם וְיוֹם רָצוֹן לַיהוָה
verses.text_kjv: Is it such a fast that I have chosen? a day for a man to afflict his soul? is it to bow down his head as a bulrush, and to spread sackcloth and ashes under him? wilt thou call this a fast, and an acceptable day to the LORD?
verses.rendering:
Is this the fast I choose —
a day for a person to humble himself?
Is it to bow your head like a reed
and spread out sackcloth and ashes?
Will you call this a fast,
a day acceptable to the LORD?
translator_notes: God does not reject humility but exposes its counterfeit: the outward posture of bowed head and sackcloth without inner transformation or outward justice.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלוֹא זֶה צוֹם אֶבְחָרֵהוּ פַּתֵּחַ חַרְצֻבּוֹת רֶשַׁע הַתֵּר אֲגֻדּוֹת מוֹטָה וְשַׁלַּח רְצוּצִים חָפְשִׁים וְכָל־מוֹטָה תְּנַתֵּקוּ
verses.text_kjv: Is not this the fast that I have chosen? to loose the bands of wickedness, to undo the heavy burdens, and to let the oppressed go free, and that ye break every yoke?
verses.rendering:
Is this not the fast I choose:
to loose the bonds of wickedness,
to untie the straps of the yoke,
to let the oppressed go free,
and to break every yoke?

key_terms.semantic_range: fast, fasting, abstinence from food, self-denial
key_terms.note: God redefines tsom ('fasting') from ritual self-denial to social justice: loosing bonds of wickedness, freeing the oppressed, sharing bread with the hungry. The true fast is not empty stomachs but full hands — the chapter's revolutionary claim.

translator_notes: This verse redefines fasting from a ritual of self-denial to an act of liberation. The four actions are all about freeing others from oppression.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלוֹא פָרֹס לָרָעֵב לַחְמֶךָ וַעֲנִיִּים מְרוּדִים תָּבִיא בָיִת כִּי־תִרְאֶה עָרֹם וְכִסִּיתוֹ וּמִבְּשָׂרְךָ לֹא תִתְעַלָּם
verses.text_kjv: Is it not to deal thy bread to the hungry, and that thou bring the poor that are cast out to thy house? when thou seest the naked, that thou cover him; and that thou hide not thyself from thine own flesh?
verses.rendering:
Is it not to share your bread with the hungry
and bring the homeless poor into your house —
when you see the naked, to cover him,
and not to turn away from your own flesh?
translator_notes: Four concrete acts of mercy: feeding, housing, clothing, and not hiding from family obligation. Jesus draws on this list in Matthew 25:35-36.
'Your own flesh' (besarkha) — the poor are not strangers but kin. All humanity shares one flesh.

verses.text_hebrew: אָז יִבָּקַע כַּשַּׁחַר אוֹרֶךָ וַאֲרֻכָתְךָ מְהֵרָה תִצְמָח וְהָלַךְ לְפָנֶיךָ צִדְקֶךָ כְּבוֹד יְהוָה יַאַסְפֶךָ
verses.text_kjv: Then shall thy light break forth as the morning, and thine health shall spring forth speedily: and thy righteousness shall go before thee; the glory of the LORD shall be thy rereward.
verses.rendering:
Then your light shall break forth like the dawn,
and your healing shall spring up swiftly.
Your righteousness shall go before you;
the glory of the LORD shall be your rear guard.
translator_notes: The 'then' is conditional — the light breaks forth only when justice replaces empty ritual. The military imagery (vanguard and rear guard) means God's righteousness and glory surround the just community.

verses.text_hebrew: אָז תִּקְרָא וַיהוָה יַעֲנֶה תְּשַׁוַּע וְיֹאמַר הִנֵּנִי אִם־תָּסִיר מִתּוֹכְךָ מוֹטָה שְׁלַח אֶצְבַּע וְדַבֶּר־אָוֶן
verses.text_kjv: Then shalt thou call, and the LORD shall answer; thou shalt cry, and he shall say, Here I am. If thou take away from the midst of thee the yoke, the putting forth of the finger, and speaking vanity;
verses.rendering:
Then you shall call, and the LORD will answer;
you shall cry, and He will say, 'Here I am.'
If you remove the yoke from your midst,
the pointing of the finger and the speaking of wickedness,
translator_notes: 'Here I am' (hineni) — the same word Abraham and Moses used in response to God. Now God uses it in response to His people. The reversal is stunning.

verses.text_hebrew: וְתָפֵק לָרָעֵב נַפְשֶׁךָ וְנֶפֶשׁ נַעֲנָה תַּשְׂבִּיעַ וְזָרַח בַּחֹשֶׁךְ אוֹרֶךָ וַאֲפֵלָתְךָ כַּצָּהֳרָיִם
verses.text_kjv: And if thou draw out thy soul to the hungry, and satisfy the afflicted soul; then shall thy light rise in obscurity, and thy darkness be as the noonday:
verses.rendering:
if you pour yourself out for the hungry
and satisfy the desire of the afflicted,
then your light shall rise in the darkness,
and your gloom shall be as the noonday.
translator_notes: 'Pour yourself out' (tapeq nafshekha) — not merely giving food but giving your very self. The fast becomes self-giving rather than self-denial.

verses.text_hebrew: וְנָחֲךָ יְהוָה תָּמִיד וְהִשְׂבִּיעַ בְּצַחְצָחוֹת נַפְשֶׁךָ וְעַצְמֹתֶיךָ יַחֲלִיץ וְהָיִיתָ כְּגַן רָוֶה וּכְמוֹצָא מַיִם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־יְכַזְּבוּ מֵימָיו
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD shall guide thee continually, and satisfy thy soul in drought, and make fat thy bones: and thou shalt be like a watered garden, and like a spring of water, whose waters fail not.
verses.rendering:
And the LORD will guide you continually
and satisfy your soul in scorched places
and strengthen your bones.
You shall be like a watered garden,
like a spring of water
whose waters do not fail.
translator_notes: The imagery reverses the fast: those who feed others are themselves fed by God. The 'watered garden' echoes Eden — justice restores paradise.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבָנוּ מִמְּךָ חָרְבוֹת עוֹלָם מוֹסְדֵי דוֹר־וָדוֹר תְּקוֹמֵם וְקֹרָא לְךָ גֹּדֵר פֶּרֶץ מְשֹׁבֵב נְתִיבוֹת לָשָׁבֶת
verses.text_kjv: And they that shall be of thee shall build the old waste places: thou shalt raise up the foundations of many generations; and thou shalt be called, The repairer of the breach, The restorer of paths to dwell in.
verses.rendering:
And your people shall rebuild the ancient ruins;
you shall raise up the foundations of many generations.
You shall be called the Repairer of the Breach,
the Restorer of Streets to Dwell In.
translator_notes: Two magnificent titles are bestowed: 'Repairer of the Breach' and 'Restorer of Streets to Dwell In.' These are vocational names — the just community is known by its restorative work.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־תָּשִׁיב מִשַּׁבָּת רַגְלֶךָ עֲשׂוֹת חֲפָצֶיךָ בְּיוֹם קָדְשִׁי וְקָרָאתָ לַשַּׁבָּת עֹנֶג לִקְדוֹשׁ יְהוָה מְכֻבָּד וְכִבַּדְתּוֹ מֵעֲשׂוֹת דְּרָכֶיךָ מִמְּצוֹא חֶפְצְךָ וְדַבֵּר דָּבָר
verses.text_kjv: If thou turn away thy foot from the sabbath, from doing thy pleasure on my holy day; and call the sabbath a delight, the holy of the LORD, honourable; and shalt honour him, not doing thine own ways, nor finding thine own pleasure, nor speaking thine own words:
translator_notes: The Sabbath is reframed as 'delight' (oneg) — not a burden of restriction but a gift of liberation from relentless productivity.

verses.text_hebrew: אָז תִּתְעַנַּג עַל־יְהוָה וְהִרְכַּבְתִּיךָ עַל־בָּמֳותֵי אָרֶץ וְהַאֲכַלְתִּיךָ נַחֲלַת יַעֲקֹב אָבִיךָ כִּי פִּי יְהוָה דִּבֵּר
verses.text_kjv: Then shalt thou delight thyself in the LORD; and I will cause thee to ride upon the high places of the earth, and feed thee with the heritage of Jacob thy father: for the mouth of the LORD hath spoken it.
verses.rendering:
then you shall delight yourself in the LORD,
and I will make you ride on the heights of the earth;
I will feed you with the heritage of Jacob your father,
for the mouth of the LORD has spoken.
translator_notes: The chapter ends where it began — with a word from the LORD's mouth. But now the word is promise rather than rebuke. The 'heritage of Jacob' is the full covenantal inheritance.
